channel.title: Debt Desk
channel.description: Daily commercial real estate debt and multifamily market briefing. Deals, SOFR and Treasury curve context, CMBS, debt funds, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and HUD execution. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk Launch: CRE Debt Risk-On, CMBS Pressure, and the Multifamily Refi Window
item.description: The Debt Desk debuts with a live read on SOFR and the Treasury curve, what is actually financing in commercial real estate debt, CMBS delinquency pressure, debt-fund and CRE CLO momentum, and where multifamily execution is opening across agency and HUD channels.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Thursday, April 2nd, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

This is our first episode, and the mission is simple. Every day, we are going to separate signal from noise in commercial real estate debt and multifamily finance. We are going to focus on what is actually getting done, where spreads are moving, where execution is real, and where the market story is getting ahead of itself.

Let’s start with the rates board, because that is still the center of gravity for everything in this business.

As of the U.S. Treasury close on April 2nd, the curve prints were roughly three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year. That means we are still in a re-steepened regime versus last year’s inversion extremes, and the key takeaway for debt desks is that the term premium is still real. If you are financing to a five-year hold, your benchmark behavior matters just as much as where the ten-year opens each morning.

On the floating side, the New York Fed’s latest SOFR print for April 1st came in at three point six five, with the thirty-day average right around three point six five as well. That steadier SOFR profile helps underwriting confidence for floating-rate bridge and transitional executions, but it does not magically fix debt service coverage where all-in coupons are still materially above legacy debt from 2021 and 2022.

So what does this mean in plain English for deals getting done right now?

It means lenders and sponsors are still closing business, but they are closing different business. The market is rewarding plans that are believable in a higher-for-longer world. It is penalizing anything that relies on perfect rent acceleration, immediate cap-rate compression, or a fast drop in benchmark rates.

You can see this in how lenders are structuring around risk. More reserves. Tighter covenants. Lower leverage at initial funding. More scrutiny around rollover schedules and expense drift. And, importantly, a lot more time spent in committee on downside liquidity, not just upside projections.

There are three financing lanes that continue to show real traction.

Lane one is refinance plus extend, where a sponsor has a decent business plan, a property with operating momentum, and enough equity to bridge the valuation gap without forcing a distressed sale. This lane is still one of the biggest sources of 2026 loan volume.

Lane two is transitional bridge debt for multifamily and selected industrial, especially when the capex path is concrete and management execution is already visible. Lenders are still open here, but they are underwriting to stabilization assumptions that are much less aggressive than they were in the low-rate cycle.

Lane three is securitized execution for the right collateral, including CRE CLO and selected CMBS channels, where liquidity has improved enough to keep transaction flow moving even as underlying property-level stress remains uneven by asset type.

On that securitization point, one of the more important data signals this quarter is the rebound in CRE CLO issuance. Trepp’s March update showed issuance at about eleven point two billion dollars as of early March, up roughly thirty-four percent versus the same point last year. That is a meaningful risk-on signal for transitional credit. It also tells you debt funds and non-bank lenders are finding enough investor demand to keep making loans.

But there is a critical nuance. This is not 2021 all over again. Underwriting assumptions today are generally more conservative, and lenders are demanding more evidence that value creation is operational, not just rate-driven. Said differently, bridge-to-bridge is still possible in pockets, but it is no longer an automatic escape hatch.

Now let’s zoom into CMBS, because the headline there still requires respect.

According to Trepp’s March 31st update, the CMBS delinquency rate rose to seven point five five percent, up forty-one basis points from February. That move reflects fresh stress across parts of the market, even as certain legacy loans continue to get modified, extended, or otherwise worked out.

For originators and capital markets teams, the practical read is this: CMBS remains open, but you need to earn your execution. Asset quality, sponsorship strength, lease profile, and refinance narrative all matter more than the broad “CMBS is back” headline.

If you are bringing a mixed-use or office-heavy story, expect deep diligence and thinner margin for underwriting errors. If you are bringing durable cash flow with clean documentation and realistic business assumptions, there is still appetite.

Now to multifamily, because this is where a lot of the 2026 debt conversation is actually being decided.

At the policy level, FHFA set 2026 multifamily purchase caps at eighty-eight billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, with at least fifty percent mission-driven and affordable focus. Workforce-housing treatment and mission carve-outs continue to matter here. The bigger picture is that agency capacity remains substantial, and the agencies are still central to market liquidity in a year with heavy refinancing needs.

From an execution standpoint, that is important for borrowers who can fit agency box requirements on quality, sponsorship, and affordability profile. For many of those deals, agencies still represent the best combination of certainty, term, and cost relative to other permanent capital options.

Freddie’s small balance channel is another signal worth tracking. Recent market commentary put the Small Balance Loan program at more than nine thousand properties and over twenty-three billion in outstanding balance, with average loan sizes well below institutional headline transactions. That tells you there is still functioning credit transmission into workforce and middle-market multifamily, not just trophy deals.

On the broader market backdrop, Fannie Mae’s March 2026 housing forecast points to moderating multifamily starts this year versus 2025, with 2027 looking more normalized. That matters because supply pressure has been one of the biggest drags on near-term rent growth in many Sun Belt markets. Slower completions over time should help rebalance fundamentals, but that process is market-specific and not immediate.

So the multifamily debt playbook right now is not one-size-fits-all. You have to segment.

In high-supply metros, lenders are still focusing hard on lease-up velocity, concessions, and burn-off assumptions. Underwrite to a slower NOI ramp and you can still get deals through. Underwrite to peak-cycle rent growth and you probably will not.

In lower-supply and Midwest-oriented submarkets, where rent growth and occupancy have held up better, execution can still be competitive, especially for stabilized assets with clean operating history.

And for value-add deals, the main question is whether your renovation plan creates real cash flow durability at a basis that supports both today’s debt service and tomorrow’s takeout options.

Let’s talk debt funds for a minute, because they continue to shape price discovery in this market.

Debt funds are often the first call for borrowers who need speed, flexibility, and custom structure. That is especially true for transitional multifamily and situations where banks are constrained or agencies are not the right fit.

The trade-off is straightforward. You can often solve complexity faster, but the coupon and structure reflect that flexibility. Today’s better outcomes are coming from sponsors who use debt funds strategically, not as a last-minute rescue. That means clear business plans, transparent reporting, and a defined refinance path before the first dollar is drawn.

When debt funds and agencies are viewed as complementary instead of competing products, transactions generally perform better. Bridge for execution certainty and value creation. Agency for durable takeout once the property is stabilized and story risk is reduced.

Now on HUD, which often gets overlooked in daily market chatter but can be a very real part of multifamily strategy.

HUD execution is not the fastest path, but it can be one of the most powerful for long-duration financing when the deal profile fits. In this environment, where long-term rate certainty still carries a premium, that matters.

There is also a compliance and operations layer that deserves attention. HUD multifamily compliance timelines tied to HOTMA implementation have been extended into 2027, which gives operators more runway on administrative adaptation, but it does not eliminate process risk. Borrowers and operators who get ahead of this now will be in a better position when timelines tighten again.

So if you are evaluating agency versus HUD for multifamily, the right question is not just “What rate can I quote today?” The right question is “What capital structure aligns with this asset’s operating reality for the next five to ten years?”

Let’s pull back to the full commercial real estate debt picture.

Mortgage Bankers Association forecasts for 2026 point to meaningful growth in commercial and multifamily originations, with a large maturity wall still feeding the pipeline. That tracks with what desks are seeing: a refinancing-driven market where execution quality, sponsor credibility, and collateral resilience are doing the heavy lifting.

This is not a broad, indiscriminate credit expansion. It is selective risk-taking. The financing is available, but it is not forgiving.

For teams actively sourcing and quoting right now, here is the practical checklist that matters most.

First, know your benchmark sensitivity. If your deal economics only work under one rate path, it is fragile.

Second, pressure-test debt service at realistic occupancy and rent assumptions, not hopeful ones.

Third, prepare your lender narrative with hard operating evidence. Better reporting wins.

Fourth, align your financing lane with your actual business plan. Do not force agency if you still have heavy transition risk. Do not force bridge if you already have stable cash flow and need durability.

Fifth, map your refinance window at closing, not twelve months later.

Now, a quick Columbus and Central Ohio lens before we close.

The local market still benefits from a structural growth narrative tied to employment expansion, logistics relevance, and long-term population support. But even in growth markets, debt is now much less tolerant of execution drift. Columbus is not immune to national capital discipline.

For local multifamily and broader CRE operators, the edge is still preparation. Deals that show clean rent rolls, realistic expenses, and a credible path to durability are finding financing. Deals that rely on broad “market growth” as a substitute for operational proof are meeting resistance.

That is especially true as capital sources compare every Midwest opportunity against a growing list of refinance needs nationwide. Your deal is no longer competing just against neighbors. It is competing against the full national maturity pipeline.

Before we wrap, here is the one thing to watch into tomorrow morning.

Watch whether front-end and belly yields continue to stabilize while CMBS and transitional credit spreads hold their recent range. If Treasury volatility stays contained and credit buyers remain engaged, we could see another week where “boring rates” quietly unlocks meaningful transaction momentum. If rates reprice sharply higher, expect committees to slow and leverage to compress again.

And one final tactical note for tomorrow: if you are sending terms, send options, not a single quote. Borrowers are moving faster when they can see a clear menu across bridge, agency, and structured alternatives in one decision frame.

That is your Debt Desk for Thursday, April 2nd, 2026.

If you are in the market today, stay disciplined, stay realistic, and keep your execution options open.

I will see you tomorrow.
